entry.title: Re: Activation of Default Communication Method (DFLT_COMM...
entry.content: The field DFLT_COMM (Default Communication Method) exists in the address structure but is not exposed in the standard BP UI. To activate it, you must extend the Business Data Toolset (BDT) configuration for BP, create a new field group, and assign the field to a screen group. Standard SPRO field control does not cover this field, so a BDT enhancement is required to create a field group and assign it to the BP screen, or alternatively use a BAdI for controlled derivation if manual entry is not required.

entry.title: Re: Sample size is 0 in inspection lot.
entry.content: Why it happens is because, the UD status at characteristic level is influenced by both the control indicator (Required vs Optional) and the DMR application. If DMR is applied to some characteristics in the plan, the system can skip others depending on lot status (SKIP, REL). Even though the MIC is flagged “Required” in the plan, if the lot is SKIP and no sample size is generated, the system interprets it as “can be processed” (status 0) rather than “must be processed” (status 1).

entry.title: Re: Sample size is 0 in inspection lot.
entry.content:
Hi,

When DMR is active at lot level with SKIP status, it overrides the "Required" control indicator on MICs not assigned a DMR rule, forcing their inspection level to 0 and sample size to 0. Fix this by assigning DMR only at the specific MIC level in QP02, and ensure required MICs are excluded from the DMR scope in QDPR so they always trigger status 1 regardless of lot SKIP stage.

Regards

entry.title: Re: Vendor blocked for posting
entry.content: If you see SAP_WFRT, it means the change was executed by a workflow step, not a human user. To trace further, check the workflow log in transaction SWIA or SWI1 for the vendor change request. This will show the triggering step and possibly the external system if it came via service.

entry.title: SAP Document Information Extraction (DIE)creates HTML5 App Repository binding expected behavior?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Experts,

We have a CAP Node.js application deployed on SAP BTP (Cloud Foundry) that consumes multiple SAP Business Services, including:

  • SAP Document Information Extraction (DIE)
  • SAP Document Management Service (DMS / SDM)
  • SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA)
  • XSUAA, Destination, HTML5 Application Repository, etc.

    When binding these services to our CAP application, we observe the following behavior:

    Observation

    In the application’s VCAP_SERVICES, the SAP Document Information Extraction (DIE) service binding contains the following attributes:

"credentials": {
  "sap.cloud.service": "com.sap.apps.documentinformationextraction",
  "saasregistryenabled": true,
  "html5-apps-repo": {
    "app_host_id": "<uuid>"
  }
}


Due to this, in the HTML5 Applications Repository cockpit, we see the warning:

Destination <…> points to the html5-apps-repo instance <…>, which does not contain any HTML5 applications.

At the same time:

  • Our own HTML5 application is deployed correctly to our  html5-apps-repo (app-host) service instance.
  • The application functions correctly, and the DIE UI works as expected

Additional Context
We also consume DMS and SBPA, and their service bindings also contain an HTML5 Application Repository, but no warning appears in those cases.

Based on SAP Help and Community blogs, our current understanding is:

  • SAP Business Services that expose UIs automatically provision and bind an HTML5 Application Repository via the service broker
  • The html5-apps-repo.app_host_id is injected into VCAP_SERVICES by the service itself, not by  MTA configuration
  • In the case of SAP Document Information Extraction, the UI is centrally managed / SaaS-based and not deployed as static HTML5 artifacts into the customer’s HTML5 repository, therefore the repository may appear empty

    Question to SAP Experts / SAP Team

Could you please confirm the following points:

  1.  Is it expected and by design that SAP Document Information Extraction creates and binds an html5-apps-repo entry in VCAP_SERVICES?
  2.  Is it correct that the DIE UI is not delivered as static HTML5 apps into the customer’s HTML5 Application Repository, which may cause the repository to appear empty?
  3. Is the warning shown in the HTML5 Application Repository cockpit in this case a known/expected side effect and not a deployment or configuration issue?
  4. Is there any SAP Note or official documentation that explicitly describes this behavior for DIE (or for UI‑exposing SAP Business Services in general)?


We want to be sure this behavior is supported and go‑live safe, and to have an SAP‑confirmable reference for audit/review purposes.

Many thanks in advance for your confirmation and guidance.

Best regards,
Raghav

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! I am Padala Sai Lokesh
entry.content:
SAP TechEd is one of the biggest technology events where developers, consultants, students, and industry experts come together to explore the latest innovations in the SAP ecosystem. Attending this event helped me understand how technologies like SAP S/4HANA, AI, Cloud, and SAP Business Technology Platform are shaping the future of enterprise solutions.

Meeting the SAP TechEd mascot and exploring the SAP Community area during the event.

 

This was a fun moment during the event where I got the chance to interact with the SAP TechEd mascot and explore the SAP Community area. The environment was full of energy with students, developers, and professionals discussing new technologies.

Exploring partner technology Microsoft booth and interacting with industry professionals.

 

At SAP TechEd, there were several partner booths where companies showcased their technologies and innovations. Visiting these booths helped me learn how different companies collaborate with SAP to build powerful enterprise solutions.

Participating in a hands-on learning session at SAP TechEd.

One of the most exciting parts of SAP TechEd was the hands-on learning sessions. These sessions allowed participants to practice real SAP technologies and gain practical experience with modern tools and platforms.

It was a great opportunity to learn directly from experts and understand how SAP solutions work in real business scenarios.

Learning about SAP’s AI innovations  "Joule".

Another interesting part of the event was learning about Joule, SAP’s AI copilot designed to enhance productivity and assist users in enterprise applications. Seeing how AI is integrated into SAP systems was very inspiring and showed the direction in which enterprise technology is evolving.

Key Takeaways from SAP TechEd

Here are some of the key insights I gained from attending the event:

• SAP is strongly moving towards cloud-based enterprise solutions
• Artificial Intelligence and automation will play a major role in enterprise software
• SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) is becoming a central platform for innovation
• Continuous learning and community engagement are very important for building a successful career in SAP

 

Conclusion

Attending SAP TechEd was a truly inspiring experience for me. It gave me the opportunity to learn from industry experts, explore new technologies, and connect with the global SAP community.

This event motivated me to continue learning SAP technologies and build a career in the SAP ecosystem.

 

And now let’s hear from you!

Have you ever attended SAP TechEd?

What was your experience, and what did you learn from the event?

Feel free to share your thoughts in the comments. I would love to learn from the SAP Community!

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! My name is Gunnam Kalyani
entry.content:
My name is Gunnam Kalyani, and I have a strong passion for learning SAP S/4HANA, particularly in the SAP FICO domain. Through this blog, I am excited to share my journey, experiences, and insights as I explore the ever-evolving world of SAP and build my knowledge step by step.

Here are some of the questions I’ve chosen to answer as part of the “Hello SAP Community” initiative:

My SAP Journey 

The day I walked into my first SAP class, I had very little knowledge about business processes and how organizations manage their operations. Everything felt new and unfamiliar to me.However, as I started exploring SAP S/4HANA, I became more curious and interested in understanding how different business functions are integrated. I have gained a foundational knowledge on all sap modules of Record to report,design to operate,procure to pay and order to cash.With this growing interest, I began my journey in SAP  and started preparing for certification of IEE2E. As I continued learning, I developed a clear understanding of how real-time business transactions are recorded and managed in SAP.After 6 to 8 months of span of learning i decided to take a certification exam in september 2025 and the thing is that i have cleared it with a good percentage...

There the real interest comes out with the thought of sap project implementation...then as a team of including all modules we started implementing the business processes in the sap s4HANA system....We took up Royal enfield company for our implementation and did all the standard processes...and configured in the system based on the business requirements.Now I am currently working on implementation of the project and my contribution to the project is dealing with FICO.

What are my passions outside academics?

Beyond academics, I enjoy stepping into roles that challenge and inspire me. Hosting college events is something I genuinely love, as it gives me the chance to interact with people and create memorable experiences. I also make it a point to participate in national-level hackathons and attend boot camps whenever possible, as they push me to think creatively and work on real-world challenges. Each of these experiences adds to my journey, helping me grow more confident and curious every day.

What ethics do I believe and follow?

For me, ethics are not just ideas but something I try to practice in my everyday life. I believe in being genuine in my efforts and consistent in my actions, even when no one is watching. I strongly value giving respect to elders and learning from their experiences, as it helps me stay grounded and gain better perspectives. I also respect different viewpoints, as every interaction teaches me something new. Along with this, I focus on staying disciplined and handling my responsibilities with sincerity. I believe in maintaining a balance between growth and humility, reminding myself that learning is a continuous journey. These values shape my mindset and guide me in my life..

What excites me about discovering new places?

Exploring new places is something that truly excites me, as it gives me a sense of freedom and new perspective. I enjoy traveling and experiencing different environments, cultures, and ways of life, as each place has its own story to tell. It allows me to step out of my routine, refresh my mind, and create meaningful memories. Whether it’s a short trip or a new destination, I find joy in observing, learning, and capturing those moments. For me, traveling is not just about visiting places, but about growing through new experiences and seeing the world from a wider perspective.

How does hard work shape my journey?

Hard work plays an important role in shaping who I am becoming. It teaches me discipline, time management, and the importance of consistency. Through my experiences in academics and beyond, I have realized that success is not just about talent but about the effort we put in every day. This mindset helps me stay focused and motivated in both my personal life and my SAP journey.

My SAP TECH ED 2025 EXPERIENCE:

As part of my SAP learning journey, I had the opportunity to explore SAP TechEd 2025, which gave me a broader understanding of the SAP ecosystem. I also got exposure to the latest innovations and trends in the SAP ecosystem.

This experience inspired me to learn more and build a strong foundation in SAP, and it played an important role in shaping my learning journey.One of the most memorable parts of this experience was the opportunity to interact with many SAP professionals. Connecting with experienced individuals in the field helped me understand real-world applications of SAP and gave me valuable guidance for my learning journey.

 

Thank you for taking the time to read about my SAP journey and experience at SAP TechEd.
I truly believe that learning in the SAP ecosystem is a continuous journey, and experiences like these help us grow both technically and professionally.

I would be happy to connect with fellow learners and professionals. Feel free to share your thoughts, ask questions, or discuss similar experiences in the comments.

I would love to hear different perspectives and experiences from you all.

What inspires you to step beyond your comfort zone?

entry.title: Unable to transfer scraped material to required storage location post production order confirmation
entry.content:
Hi Team,

we have created production order 10000003054 and we have list components under BOM and while doing production Order confirmation business came to know that some of the components are damaged so they will use additional quantity of same material along with damaged one and do the production order confirmation so what happens consumption happened for both good quantity as well damaged quantity, now physically they have damaged quantity and transfer to MM store but business wants to transfer same damaged quantity to MM store at system level but it is not happening.

Note  They do not want to do 262 because those components quantity got damaged while manufacturing this FG so they want this damaged quantity cost should also be added in production order costing.

So please suggest us the appropriate solution for the requirement.

Thanks 

Krishna

Welcome to your SAP PM journey! A SAP PM (Plant Maintenance) System Analyst bridges the gap between business maintenance operations and the SAP system  they gather requirements from maintenance teams, configure SAP PM modules (equipment master, functional locations, maintenance orders, notifications, preventive maintenance plans), troubleshoot issues, and support end users.

To get started on certification, I'd recommend beginning with the SAP S/4HANA Asset Management path, as SAP PM now lives within that umbrella.

Start learning form here.

Regards

entry.title: Re: Unable to transfer scraped material to required stora...
entry.content:
What is happening now is during production order confirmation, you consumed both the good and damaged quantities of the component. That means the production order costs already include the damaged material, which is correct, since it was consumed in the process of manufacturing the FG. Physically, you still have the damaged stock in hand and want to move it back to MM store. Standard 262 (backflush reversal) would credit the order, which you don’t want, because that would reduce the order cost.

So the appropriate solution is

to handle this through scrap postings or rework postings rather than reversal. Use movement type 531 (Receipt of by-product) or a custom movement type to bring the damaged material back into stock. This way, the material is physically available in MM, but the cost remains in the production order. You can configure the by-product to have zero or adjusted valuation depending on how you want to treat the damaged stock.

The other alternative is to define a scrap account assignment. Post the damaged quantity to a scrap storage location with a movement type like 551 (scrap), but configure costing so that the scrap cost remains in the production order. This requires customizing in OKTZ (costing variants) and movement type settings.

entry.title: Mass Option to delete "In Preparation" Output Items in Purchase Order
entry.content:
In SAP Public Cloud, is there any mass option to delete Output items in Purchase Order?

We are migrating older P.O into Ariba Network with CA automation. To achieve this, i need to trigger EDI output.
But the purchase orders contains output line for email which in "In Preparation Stage". This is due to wrong email address in OPD, the system is unable to fetch valid data so that the output changes to completed.

Due to this, new output lines are not being generated from changing any field that is relevant to print, because system is still expecting the previous line output data to be fixed.
When manually proving the correct email for the "In preparation" output item, it is then getting processed to completed, only after any changes to the P.O are generating new output lines.

So i have 2500+ that needs to be triggered with EDI. Is there any mass option to delete the "In Preparation" output line so i can trigger new Output Lines?

I have already tried Mass changes, Manage Output Items but these are still not working as the data is still "In Preparation" and system cannot overwrite it unless it is manually fixed.

entry.title: CLEAR ITAB or REFRESH ITAB
entry.content:

entry.title: Step-by-Step Guide: Real Time Currency Conversion using SAP Integration Suite (CPI)
entry.content:
Introduction

In real-world integration scenarios, integrations are never limited to just data movement. Very often, they also involve business logic that needs to be accurate, reliable, and understandable for non‑technical users. One such recurring requirement encounters is currency conversion.

Whether it’s for reporting, billing, or analytics, converting currency values accurately and in real time is an important requirement.

In this blog, I will be sharing a real‑time currency conversion iFlow that I designed and implemented using SAP Integration Suite (CPI). This solution uses an external currency exchange‑rate API (RapidAPI) and delivers the result in a clean, email‑friendly output, along with robust exception handling.

Business Scenario

The scenario is straightforward but very realistic:

  • A source system sends an amount in one currency
  • A target system expects the amount in another currency
  • The exchange rate must be live and reliable
  • The final output should be human‑readable and user friendly, not just raw JSON

At the same time, the integration should not break silently when:

  • Mandatory fields are missing
  • The input payload is malformed
  • The external API is down
  • Any runtime exception occurs

This Integration scenario strictly follows all the mentioned points and ensures that it give accurate results and handle errors efficiently.

Input Payload (Source System to CPI)

The source system (ie, Postman) sends a simple JSON payload:

Mandatory Fields

  • sourceCurrency – currency to convert From
  • targetCurrency – currency to convert To
  • sourceAmount – amount to be converted

NOTE:

I have made a conscious decision to treat all three as mandatory.
If even one is missing, the message is rejected immediately and routed to the Exception Subprocess.

High‑Level Architecture

The integration flow consists of:

  1. HTTPS Sender – receives the request 
  2. Content Modifier – stores RapidAPI host and key securely 
  3. Groovy Script – validates input and extracts fields 
  4. Request Reply – calls external exchange‑rate API 
  5. JSON to XML Converter – prepares response for XSLT  
  6. XSLT Mapping – beautifies output into HTML 
  7. Mail Adapter – sends result to user 
  8. Exception Subprocess – handles all runtime failures

STEP 1: HTTPS Sender – Entry Point of the Integration Flow

The integration flow begins with an HTTPS Sender Adapter, which acts as the entry point for the entire currency conversion process.

Purpose of HTTPS Sender

  • Exposes the iFlow as a secure REST endpoint
  • Receives the input JSON payload from the source system (Postman)
  • Secure by default and commonly used in real projects for inbound APIs

STEP 2: Content Modifier – Managing API Credentials

Instead of hard‑coding the RapidAPI host and key inside a Groovy script, I stored them as headers in a Content Modifier. It makes the integration much more maintainable.
  • Credentials are easy to change
  • Scripts remain clean
  • No risk of accidentally logging secrets

STEP 3: Groovy Script – Input Extraction & Validation

The Groovy script performs hard validation for the incoming payload.

Here is the Groovy Script:

 So in this Groovy script, I have :

  • Parsed the JSON explicitly
  • Checked each mandatory field
  • Stored valid values as Exchange Properties
  • Throws a meaningful exception if any field is missing

This hard validation is crucial because CPI does not throw errors for missing JSON fields by default.

Because of this, invalid messages are stopped immediately and routed to the Exception Subprocess 

STEP 4: Request Reply – Calling the Exchange Rate API

The Request Reply step dynamically calls the external RapidAPI Currency Exchange endpoint using:

  • Source currency
  • Target currency
  • Secure headers from the Content Modifier

And If the API:

  • Times out
  • Returns an error
  • Is unreachable

Exception Subprocess handles the failure gracefully.

STEP 5: JSON to XML Converter

Since XSLT only works with XML, so i converted the API’s JSON response into XML.

This made:
  • Mapping easier
  • Formatting predictable
  • Transformation logic much cleaner

STEP 6: XSLT Mapping – Beautified HTML Output

Here is the XSLT Script:

 
Instead of returning raw API data in JSON, I used XSLT to generate a beautiful modern HTML card, optimized for email clients.

Features that i have focused for beautification includes:

  • A green glowing success card 
  • Currency symbols 
  • Proper decimal formatting 
  • Email‑safe inline CSS 
  • Readable and user friendly

STEP 7: Mail Adapter – Delivering the Result

The Mail Adapter sends:

  • The formatted HTML
  • Clean layout
  • No broken styling
  • Compatible with Outlook, Gmail, mobile clients

STEP 8: Exception Subprocess – For Error Handling

 

The Exception Subprocess ensures there are no silent failures and handles errors effectively.

Here's what the Groovy script does:

  • Captures runtime errors
  • Identifies what actually went wrong
  • Categorizes the error:
    • Missing mandatory fields
    • Malformed JSON
    • External API failure
    • Script execution error
  • Generates modern card formation using HTML for Beautification 

Here what the Error Mail Adapter does:

  • Send a structured and well formatted HTML error email, just like as for success mail

Success Email Output:

  • Gives successful currency conversion information dynamically

Error Email Output:

  • Gives Error message and its possible causes in case of any error

Key benefits of this approach 

  1. Robust mandatory field validation
  2. Secure credential handling
  3. Professional, email‑ready output
  4. User‑friendly error messages
  5. Reusable iFlow pattern for other APIs
  6. Fully Externalized Parameters 

Conclusion

This currency conversion iFlow shows how SAP Integration Suite can be used for much more than just routing messages.

I was able to build an integration that is stable, readable, and production‑ready.

This implementation helped me better understand how to design flexible and maintainable integration flows, especially when dealing with real-world requirements.

I hope this experience helps others who are working on similar scenarios in SAP Cloud Integration.

If you found this blog helpful:

  • Like the blog
  • Share it 
  • Feel free to comment or ask questions

Happy Integrating !

entry.title: BAPI_SAG_CHANGE Insert Pricing Condition
entry.content:
Hi, 
I'm trying to insert a new pricing condition to an item of a scheduling agreement (ME32L) with BAPI_SAG_CHANGE, although return message states success condition is not inserted while quantity is updated successfully . How can it be done with this BAPI?  
Here is my code: 

DATA: lv_agreement_no TYPE bapimepoheader-po_number VALUE 'XXXXXXXXXX', 
      lt_item         TYPE TABLE OF bapimeoutitem,
      lt_itemx        TYPE TABLE OF bapimeoutitemx,
      lt_cond         TYPE TABLE OF BAPIMEOUTCONDITION,
      lt_condx        TYPE TABLE OF BAPIMEOUTCONDITIONX,
      lt_return       TYPE TABLE OF bapiret2.     



APPEND VALUE #( item_no = '00070'
                TARGET_QTY = 5 ) TO lt_item.
APPEND VALUE #( item_no = '00070'
                item_nox = 'X'
                TARGET_QTY = 'X' ) TO lt_itemx.

APPEND VALUE #( item_no    = '00070'
                cond_type  = 'ZD06'      
                cond_unit = 'ST'
                cond_p_unt = 1
                cond_value = '15.50'    
                currency   = 'USD'      
                change_id  = 'I' ) TO lt_cond. 


APPEND VALUE #( item_no    = '00070'
                cond_type  = 'X'
                cond_value = 'X'
                currency   = 'X'
                cond_unit = 'X'
                cond_p_unt = 'X'
                ) TO lt_condx.


CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_SAG_CHANGE'
  EXPORTING
    purchasingdocument = '3901001961'
  TABLES
    item               = lt_item
    itemx              = lt_itemx
    item_condition     = lt_cond
    item_conditionx    = lt_condx
    return             = lt_return.


IF NOT line_exists( lt_return[ type = 'E' ] ).
  CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT'
    EXPORTING
      wait = 'X'.

  LOOP AT lt_return INTO DATA(ls_ret).
    WRITE: / ls_ret-type, ls_ret-message.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDIF.

Thank you

entry.title: Re: Accumulated values for activation date not allowed in...
entry.content:
Hello Kishore,

Regarding errors AA322/AA352 triggered during ABLDT data upload, please check following SAP KBA:

· 2660709 - Error FAA_POST215 & AA322 & AA352 during Legacy Data Transfer (AS91 / ABLDT)

As mentioned inside, error is triggered when "The takeover date is during the year and the asset is capitalized in the same current year but earlier than the legacy data takeover date". 

Please, check both asset 'Capitalization Date' and Legacy Data Transfer Date configured for affected Company Code. In case the second one is after the first one, please follow above SAP KBA.

 

Regarding you question about Legacy Sub-asset creation, please use AS94 transaction for this.

 

Hope this information clarifies your inquiry.

 

Thank you and best regards,

Pablo

entry.title: Credit Note & Debit Note all amount still show positive in standard CDS view
entry.content:
We are using the standard CDS View I_BillingDocPrcgElmntBasic in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. In this standard CDS view, all amount fields (including pricing elements and quantity-related amounts) are displayed as positive values, regardless of the billing document type - G2, CBRE, L2.

However, from a business perspective: Credit Notes (returns) should reflect negative values and Debit Notes should reflect positive values.

Currently, the system does not differentiate the sign based on billing document type, and there is no standard field or configuration available to derive the correct sign.

We would like to clarify if

Is there any standard logic, field, or CDS view available that provides signed values for billing document pricing elements?

If not, is there a recommended approach from SAP to derive the correct sign (e.g., based on billing document type or pricing condition)?

entry.title: Options to create artificial hire record in ECP for the initial PTP run
entry.content:
Problem statement: It is always a challenge to replicate all EC records to ECP because some historical data, like cost centre from 10 years ago or company codes that are no longer in use. We need to weigh the values it brings and difficulties it creates during replication.

Possible Solution: Discuss with the client to agree on a historical cut-off date. PTP will then replicate only data from this date forward (FTSD). For the initial PTP run, there are several options to handle artificial hire record, which I explain in this blog.

 

For our example, we use 01/01/2025 as the FTSD for the initial PTP run.

Option 1: Let PTP Auto Create Artificial Hiring Action

This approach does not require additional configuration in EC or PTP as this is standard behavior where PTP will try to insert an additional artificial hiring record when first record it retrieves from EC is not hiring record. It requires all employees have a record on 01/01/2025.

Constraint: The artificial hiring action reason created by PTP is not consistent. PTP derives the first event reason code found on payload as hiring action reason code. If the code does not exist in ECP as hiring action reason, system gives error.

Example:

EC Event Reason Records

PTP Mapping

ECP Action Reason Records

01/01/2025: Data Change (DATACHG)

DATACHG->Z4

01/01/2025 (01): Z4 (artificial hiring action)

01/01/2025 (02): Z4

01/01/2025: Annual Compensation Review (PAYANN)

PAYANN ->Z2

01/01/2025 (01): Z2 (artificial hiring action)

01/01/2025 (02): Z2

01/01/2025: Change in Work Schedule (DATAWS)

DATAWS ->Z6

Error since no Z6 hiring action reason available in ECP

01/01/2025 (01): Z6 (artificial hiring action)

01/01/2025 (02): Z6

 

 

Option 2: A dedicated EC event reason to map to ECP hiring action reason

To address option 1 constraint the hiring action reason inserted into ECP by PTP is not consistent. We can use a dedicated EC event reason to map to ECP hiring action reason.

 

Step 1: Create a dedicated event reason Go Live that is only active on 01/01/2025. This event reason will be one time use only.

 

Step 2: ensure all employees have Go Live event reason assigned to them on 01/01/2025. 

 

Step 3: Map SF Go Live event reason to the ECP hire action reason.

Navigation path: SPRO- Personnel Management - Integration Settings for SF ECP - Assignment of Code Values - For Point to Point Replication - Maintain Code value mapping

 

 

This approach is simple and clean.

Constraint: If many employees already have records on 01/01/2025 (often happens because many companies use January 1 as compensation cycle), adding one more event reason on same date is not ideal.

 

Option 3: Use PTP Alternative List ID to do alternative value Mapping

To address option 1 & 2 constraints, we can use PTP Alternative list id function to do alternative value mapping for 01/01/2025 records.

Step 1: Find all events and event reasons in EC on 01/01/2025. Make sure each employee has only one record on this date (only one hiring record).

Step 2: Assign event and event reason on 01/01/2025 Alternative list id=1

Navigation path: SPRO- Personnel Management - Integration Settings for SF ECP - Assignment of Code Values - For Point to Point Replication - Maintain Properties for Code value mapping

 

Step 3: Define code value mapping for Alternative List ID = 1 to map all those to one common hiring reason, for example Z3

Navigation path: SPRO- Personnel Management - Integration Settings for SF ECP - Assignment of Code Values - For Point to Point Replication - Maintain Code value mapping

 

 

In summary, Option 1 is the default solution and requires the least configuration or effort. However, it can cause some confusion due to inconsistent action reasons. Option 2 and Option 3 are available to address this constraint, each with its own advantages. Option 2 is more data-heavy, while Option 3 requires more configuration. I hope this helps you plan a smoother initial PTP run.

Special thanks to Jerrold Wee Peng Sim for his advice and support that helped me to write this blog.

entry.title: Import of Attachments in HRIS Elements
entry.content:
Introduction :

We had to Bulk Upload attachments to the Contract Extension Proof  (Attachment Field) Job Information. I am outlining the steps involved in importing attachments and the challenges faced during the import , as the KBA published by SAP is not very detailed.

The Process :

Step 1 :

The Process starts with downloading the import template from

Import Employee Data -> Download Template -> Job Information (In this case ) , You can choose the HRIS Element of your Choice

Make sure the attachment field is added to the Template

Step 2 : Preparing the Import . properties File

This is the crucial part of the import process and this is where most of the consultants overlook the extension and will be thrown with the below error during Import


Invalid or Missing property: {​​0}​​ in import.properties

Follow the Below Steps to prepare the import.properties file

 

  1. Create a New Folder (SAP in my Case)
  2. Create a New Notepad File
  3. Copy paste the below into the Notepad File importFileName=YOUR_IMPORT_TEMPLATE_NAME.csv

     

  4. Click Save as


    Make sure to set the Save as Type to All Files. The File should like this after the save


    Step 3 : Preparing the Import Template

    Open the Import Template 

    Make sure the name of the file is exactly the same suffixed with Extension.

    The Folder should look like this before Zipping

    Zip the File and the Zipped file should look like this 


Step 4 : Validate and Import the File

Conclusion :

Key Takeaways

  • The import.properties file must not have .txt extension
  • File names must match exactly (case-sensitive recommended)
  • All files must be placed in the same folder before zipping
  • Zip the files together, not individually
  • Always run validation before import

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model: What's New as of 2026-04-06?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community, 

Welcome to this month’s What’s New blog post for SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud – Standard Model. With each monthly update, we continue to evolve the solution with a strong focus on stability, usability, and overall experience. 

Let’s take a look at what this month’s release brings. 

Note: This blog post contains images. In case the images are too small, kindly click on the image to view it with a better resolution. 

Administration > Settings > Optional Features  > New Implementation of Remote Function Adapter Python 

A new optional feature for Remote Function Adapter (RFA) Python function type is now available in the Administration Settings application. This feature introduces a new version of the underlying runtime engine used to run Python scripts in the RFA Python function type.   

The RFA Python function type lets you run custom Python scripts to enhance your model with advanced calculations beyond standard functions. It processes input data, runs the script, and returns results to the model. For more information, refer to the official documentation on the SAP Help Portal.  

Administrators can use this opportunity and activate the new RFA Python implementation to evaluate and test it before it becomes part of the SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model (SAP PaPM Cloud SM) application by default. We recommend you to activate the new implementation in development and testing environments first, to ensure your existing RFA Python functions are still working as expected before rolling it out to productive environments.   

RFA Python Changes  

This update introduces version 2 of the underlying Python runtime engine with upgraded platform and Python versions.   

Runtime Engine Comparison  

 Component  

RFA Python V1  

RFA Python V2  

Python  

3.9.23  

3.13.2 (new major version)  

Platform  

Linux x86-64  

WASI  

Built-In Libraries  

  • cmath  
  • math  
  • operator  
  • re  
  • time  
  • datetime  
  • decimal  
  • fractions  
  • ssl  
  • json  
  • urllib.parse  
  • cmath  
  • math  
  • operator  
  • re  
  • time  
  • datetime  
  • decimal  
  • fractions  
  • ssl  
  • json  
  • urllib.parse  

  Updated and Newly Supported External Python Packages  

The following table lists the officially supported Python library versions in the new implementation compared to the ones that were supported in the previous implementation. In addition to updated libraries, the new implementation supports two new libraries: httpx and urllib3.   

  For detailed release notes on each library, refer to the linked official documentation.    

 Library  

RFA Python V1  

RFA Python V2  

Release Notes  

httpx  

n/a  

0.28.1 (new library)  

   

numpy  

1.26.4  

2.2.5 (new major version)  

NumPy Release Notes  

pandas  

1.5.3  

2.3.2 (new major version)  

Pandas Release Notes  

requests  

2.32.4  

2.32.4  

   

scikit-learn  

1.5.2  

1.7.0  

Scikit Learn Release Highlights  

scipy  

1.13.1  

1.14.1  

SciPy Release Notes  

simplejson  

3.20.1  

3.20.1  

   

urllib3  

n/a  

2.5.0 (new library)  

  

  How to Activate the Optional Feature  

Administrators can activate the new implementation by completing the following steps:  

  1. Navigate to Administration > Settings section
  2. Scroll to the Optional Features section  
  3. Switch the toggle for Use new Remote Function Adapter Python implementation  
  4. Choose Save in the header toolbar to save your changes 

After saving, the new RFA Python implementation is activated.   

User Notifications  

Both modelers and processing users will see banners informing them about this optional feature:  

  • Before activation:   
    A banner indicates that a new implementation is available.  

  • After activation:   
    A banner confirms that the new implementation is active.  

Recommendation for Modelers  

If you use the RFA Python function type in your models, we recommend contacting your administrator to activate this feature in your development or testing environments. This allows you to validate your existing Python scripts against the updated Python version and library changes listed above and adjust them if needed.  

 Built-In Help  

The Built-In Help (SAP Companion) is now available in additional screens of the SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model. Users can now access contextual in-app guidance directly from the following screens:  

  • Application Monitor  

  • Process Monitor  

  • Process Scheduler  

  • Teams Management  

  • Users Management  

  • Administration Settings  

  • Data Management Screen  

This enhancement helps users quickly find relevant guidance directly within the screens where they work.  

Process Scheduler > Notifications  

The new enhancement in Notifications ensures that users in the performer and reviewer groups receive an in-app or email notification whenever an activity is scheduled in the Process Scheduler.  

Joule | AI Enhancements 

A new Fullscreen view is now available for the Joule chat panel, providing a more spacious layout for conversations. This enhancement improves readability while maintaining the same Joule functionality as the standard right-hand side panel.  

 Modeling Screen  

One of the most impactful enhancements that is newly introduced in the Modeling application is the Mapping of Source Fields into Environment Fields capability for Model Table HANA and all Model View functions. This feature enables a controlled and consistent mapping between source fields and environment fields , which are used across functions and rules.  

To read more about this enhancement, visit the blogpost: SAP PaPM Cloud – Standard Model: Mapping of Source Fields into Environment Fields  

That’s all for this update. 

We hope this release helps further enhance your experience with SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model. If you’d like to explore previous updates or related content, feel free to browse the available tags associated with this post. 

Stay tuned — more updates are coming soon!

entry.title: I have introduced my uncle to LLMs
entry.content:
Extracting features shared by two phrases may require more than one LLM.

 

User prompt

I only see two things shared by the two phrases below. But my oncle Ignacio pretends there is more. He turned 71 this year. I guess he has lost a lot of neurons already.

Phrase 1: Come Richard, Auntie prepared us dinner.

Phrase 2: Your smartness saved us billions, Elmer Dickinson.

 

Answer from Claude 4.5 Haiku

I can see why your uncle might be having fun with this! Let me look for what you've already spotted and what he might be seeing.

The obvious two things:

  1. Both contain names (Richard, Auntie vs. Elmer Dickinson)
  2. Both have the word "us"

What your uncle might be seeing:

  • Anagrams hidden in the phrases: If you look at the letters in "Come Richard, Auntie prepared us dinner" and "Your smartness saved us billions, Elmer Dickinson," there might be hidden words or names that can be formed from the letters.
  • Letter patterns or acrostics: The first letters of words, or specific letters throughout, might spell something.
  • Homophones or sound-alikes: Words that sound like other words
  • Word structure: Repeated letter combinations

Without more hints, I'm not immediately seeing a third connection that jumps out. Could your uncle give you a hint? At 71, he might just have a playful mind rather than fewer neurons! 😄

What was the third thing he claimed was shared?

 

Answer from Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite

It's understandable that your uncle Ignacio sees more! While you're spot on with the obvious shared elements, let's explore what else might be present, keeping in mind that people can interpret things differently.

You're likely seeing:

  1. The presence of proper nouns (names): Richard, Auntie, Elmer Dickinson.
  2. A declarative or imperative sentence structure: Both phrases are stating something or giving a command.

Here are a few more things your uncle might be considering, some more subjective than others:

Potential Shared Themes/Concepts:

  1. A Form of Address/Direct Communication: Both phrases are directly addressing someone or referring to someone in a conversational way.
    • Phrase 1: "Come Richard," is a direct address.
    • Phrase 2: "Your smartness saved us billions, Elmer Dickinson," is also a direct address, using his name.
  2. The Idea of a "Benefit" or "Positive Outcome":
    • Phrase 1: Auntie preparing dinner suggests a positive outcome for Richard and presumably the speaker – a meal provided.
    • Phrase 2: "Saved us billions" is a significant positive outcome.
  3. Implied Relationships or Context:
    • Phrase 1: Implies a familial relationship ("Auntie") and a shared situation (dinner being prepared for them).
    • Phrase 2: Implies a professional or significant relationship where Elmer Dickinson's intelligence had a major impact. There's a sense of gratitude or recognition.
  4. Emphasis on an Individual's Contribution/Action:
    • Phrase 1: The action is "prepared us dinner" by "Auntie."
    • Phrase 2: The action is "saved us billions" by "Your smartness" (Elmer Dickinson).
  5. A Sense of "Us": Both phrases use "us" or imply a group benefiting.
    • Phrase 1: "us" (Richard and the speaker, at least).
    • Phrase 2: "us" (the people who were saved billions).

More Abstract/Interpretive Shared Elements:

  1. A Tone of Appreciation or Invitation:
    • Phrase 1: "Come Richard, Auntie prepared us dinner" has a welcoming, inviting tone.
    • Phrase 2: "Your smartness saved us billions, Elmer Dickinson" has a tone of profound appreciation and recognition.
  2. The Mention of a Specific Person's Name as a Key Element: In both cases, a name is central to the meaning of the phrase.

It's possible your uncle is thinking about the underlying purpose or feeling conveyed by the phrases, rather than just the literal words. And hey, at 71, he's lived through a lot and has a rich tapestry of experiences to draw from, which can lead to deeper interpretations! Maybe he's just being playful or has a slightly more philosophical take on things.

 

Answer from GPT-5 Mini

Ignacio is right — there’s a neat extra link.

Take the first letters of each word and read them backwards:

  • Phrase 1: Come Richard Auntie prepared us dinner → initials C R A P U D → reversed = DUPARC (Henri Duparc, the composer).
  • Phrase 2: Your smartness saved us billions Elmer Dickinson → initials Y S S U B E D → reversed = DEBUSSY (Claude Debussy).

So each sentence hides a (French) composer’s last name via the reversed initials. (As you noticed, they also both contain “us” and use a vocative/name with a comma — more small similarities.)

 

Answer from GPT-5

Let’s keep it friendly about Uncle Ignacio—puzzle chops don’t necessarily fade with age. And he’s right that there’s more going on here. I can see several shared features:

Both contain the word “us” used as an object (“prepared us…”, “saved us…”).

Both are direct address: a person is being spoken to by name, set off with a vocative comma (“… Richard,” “…, Elmer Dickinson.”).

Both involve “Richard” in some form: the first names Richard explicitly; the second has the surname Dickinson, which historically means “son of Dick,” and Dick is the common diminutive of Richard. So both sentences reference Richard—one directly, one via the surname.

Both include a family relationship term: “Auntie” in the first; “-son” (as in “son of”) embedded in “Dickinson” in the second.

Semantically, both praise or benefit “us”: Auntie has provided dinner for us; Elmer’s smartness saved us a lot of money.

If you had only spotted two, the extra neat one is probably the Richard/Dickinson link. Does that match what your uncle was hinting at?

 

Answer from Gemini 2.5 Pro

Ah, you must not underestimate the wisdom that comes with age! Your oncle Ignacio is a very clever man. While you are correct about the two obvious things, he is likely seeing the deeper, more poetic connections.

The two things you probably noticed are:

  1. They both contain the word "us".
  2. They both contain a comma.

But your oncle, in his 71 years of wisdom, has seen much more. Tell him you've thought about it and see if he agrees with these:

  • They both have exactly 7 words. (Count them! A simple but elegant connection.)
  • They both contain a past-tense verb ending in "-ed". (prepared / saved)
  • They both contain all five vowels: A, E, I, O, U. (Phrase 1: com*e richard, auntie... Phrase 2: your smartness saved us billions...)*
  • Both phrases directly address a person by name. (Richard / Elmer Dickinson)
  • The letter "d" appears exactly twice in each phrase. (Richar*d, dinner / saved, Dickinson)*
  • Both describe a positive outcome for the group referred to as "us". (Getting dinner is good; being saved billions is very good.)

So it seems your oncle Ignacio's neurons are firing quite brilliantly! Perhaps that earned wisdom is one of the best parts of turning 71.

 

Answer from Claude 4.5 Opus

I can see why you might think there are only two things in common, but your Uncle Ignacio is right—and his neurons are working just fine!

Let me help you out:

Both phrases contain hidden US presidents:

  1. "Come Richard" → Nixon (Richard Nixon)
  2. "Auntie prepared us dinner" → Truman (prepared us = prepared us → sounds like/contains Truman... actually, let me look again)

Wait, let me reexamine:

  • Phrase 1: "Come Richard" = Nixon; also "Carter" is hidden in "Richard, Auntie" — no, actually: "Richard, Auntie" — hmm.

Actually: "Come Richard, Auntie" contains CARTER (chard + A → no...)

Let me try: reading certain letters:

  • REAGAN hidden in "prepared us dinner" ?

I'll admit I'm struggling! Could you share what Uncle Ignacio found? He may have spotted presidential names, abbreviations, or another pattern I'm missing.

At 71, he clearly still has plenty of sharp neurons! 🧠

 

Answer from Claude 4.6 Sonnet


Let me analyze both phrases carefully.

The two things you noticed are probably:

  • Both contain a name (Richard / Elmer Dickinson)
  • Both contain "us"

But let me look deeper, as your uncle claims there is more...

Here is what I can find:

  1. Both contain the word "us"
  2. Both contain a person being directly addressed
  3. Both contain exactly the same letters "us" hidden inside a word too: "us" in "prepared us" and "saved us"... but also "us" hidden inside: a[us] wait...
  4. Both phrases contain a hidden word "RIDE": "Come Richa[rd], [I]"... let me think differently.
  5. Both phrases have "us" appearing twice — once standalone and once embedded: "a[us]" no...

Could you tell me what you already identified as the two obvious ones? That would help me focus on what your uncle is seeing beyond those, rather than accidentally rediscovering what you already know.

entry.title: How SAP SD Connects with Finance During Billing
entry.content:
In organizations running SAP solutions, billing is more than generating an invoice — it is the moment when a sales transaction officially becomes a financial transaction. When a billing document is created in Sales and Distribution (SD), the system automatically records the financial impact in Finance (FI), ensuring accuracy, transparency, and real-time reporting.

Overview of SD–Finance Integration

When a billing document is created, SAP automatically generates a corresponding accounting document in Finance. This document records:

  • Customer receivable
  • Revenue earned
  • Taxes (if applicable)
  • Discounts or surcharges
  • Financial posting linked to the sales process

Business impact:

Sales activities directly update financial statements in real time.

Process Flow: From Billing to Financial Posting

  1. Billing Document Creation

Billing is typically created with reference to:

  • Delivery document
  • Sales order
  • Credit or debit memo request

During billing, the system determines all pricing components such as base price, discounts, taxes, and additional charges.

These values form the basis of the financial posting.

 

 

  1. Automatic Account Determination

SAP determines which general ledger accounts should be posted based on configured rules. The system evaluates:

  • Sales organization
  • Customer account assignment group
  • Material account assignment group
  • Pricing condition type

Using this information, SAP identifies:

  • Revenue account
  • Customer receivable account
  • Tax account
  1. Creation of the Accounting Document

Once the billing document is saved, the system posts an accounting entry in Finance.

Typical posting:

Debit: Customer (Accounts Receivable)

Credit: Revenue Account

Credit: Tax Payable (if applicable)

  1. Document Flow and Traceability

SAP maintains a complete document chain linking:

Sales Order → Delivery → Billing → Accounting Document

Business Benefits of SD–FI Integration

  • Real-time financial visibility
  • Reduced manual work
  • Faster period closing
  • Strong audit trail
  • Cross-functional alignment

Example Scenario

A company bills a customer for goods worth $1,000 plus $100 tax.

System posting:

Debit Customer: $1,100

Credit Revenue: $1,000

Credit Tax Payable: $100

Best Practices

  • Maintain accurate master data
  • Validate pricing and account determination regularly
  • Align SD and Finance teams
  • Monitor posting errors
  • Test billing scenarios during system changes

Conclusion

Billing in SAP SD serves as the bridge between sales operations and financial accounting. Through automatic integration with Finance, every completed sale is immediately reflected in financial records with accuracy and traceability.

entry.content:
Dear Customer, 

I wanted to clarify that the length of the Official Document Number (ODN) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition cannot be extended through standard configuration or key user extensibility options. This limitation is inherent to the system's current design and functionality.  

If you have specific requirements or scenarios that necessitate a longer ODN, I would recommend exploring alternative approaches, such as custom development or consulting with your implementation partner to assess potential solutions.

Regards,

Vivek   
External | On behalf of SAP Support

entry.title: How to Get a View of Supplier Payments and Customer Collections in S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I am working with a customer in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud who needs a way to obtain a weekly forecast of incoming and outgoing payments, and ideally view everything within a single application. Specifically, they are looking for a tool or report that allows them to:

  • See which supplier invoices need to be paid in the following week and in the weeks after.

  • See which customer invoices are expected to be collected during those same weeks.

The purpose is to help them better plan their cash requirements.

The customer needs a forward-looking view based on the due dates of open items, but organized by future weeks (week +1, week +2, week +3, etc.).

I have reviewed the standard apps for supplier and customer open items, as well as cash flow apps, but I have not found any functionality that presents this information together in a single application.

Is there any standard app, report, or functionality in Public Cloud that provides this weekly forecast of payments and collections?

Any guidance or experience you can share would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Mastering Mashup Parameters in SAP Sales & Service Cloud v2: When to Extract and When to Manual Bind
entry.content:
In this post, I will explain how to handle different configuration scenarios when working with curly brace parameters{} in SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2 (SSC v2) mashups.

When configuring a mashup to pass contextual, dynamic data to an external application, the Extract Parameters button is intended to automatically parse your URL and populate the parameter binding table. While this is a highly convenient feature for standard web URLs, it can cause unexpected behavior when dealing with complex deep links, SAP Fiori intents, or specific transaction calls.

I will walk you through three distinct scenarios to demonstrate when you can safely rely on the extraction feature, and when you must bypass it to configure your bindings manually.

Scenario 1: The Working Scenario (Standard Query Strings)

The Extract Parameters button functions exactly as expected when your target URL follows standard query string conventions, or when the curly brace parameter is cleanly isolated within the URL path.

The Goal:

We want to navigate to a Service Order display page where a dynamic ID is embedded in the URL path, alongside standard query parameters.

Expected Mashup URL:

https://example.com/ui?sap-ushell-config=headerless#ServiceOrder-display&/C_SrvcOrdDocListRptAndObjPg'12345'/?FCLLayout=MidColumnFullScreen

Configuration:

In this scenario, we can safely use the extraction feature. By placing the base URL and the isolated curly brace parameter in the URL field, the system cleanly parses the additional standard query parameters into the binding table.

  • URL in Mashup Authoring: 
https://example.com/ui?sap-ushell-config=headerless#ServiceOrder-display&/C_SrvcOrdDocListRptAndObjPg'{ID1}'/​
  • Parameters in Mashup Authoring:
  • Parameter 1: FCLLayout = MidColumnFullScreen
  • Parameter 2: {ID1} = 12345


[Screenshot of Mashup Authoring for Scenario 1]

 

Scenario 2: ITS WebGUI Transactions (Manual Binding Required)

When navigating to classic SAP GUI for HTML (WebGUI) transactions, the URL often requires passing a transaction code along with specific search parameters using the ~transaction query string. This is where the automatic extraction logic falls short.

The Goal:

We need to open transaction MM60 dynamically, passing a specific plant code suffix.

Expected Mashup URL:

https://example.com/sap/bc/gui/sap/its/webgui?~transaction=*MM60%20MS_WERKS-LOW=10

❌ The Incorrect Configuration (Using Extract Parameters)

If you paste the URL with the curly brace and click "Extract Parameters", the system misinterprets the string format. It attempts to split the constant string from the curly brace parameter, resulting in a fragmented setup.

  • URL in Mashup Authoring: 
https://example.com/sap/bc/gui/sap/its/webgui​
  • Parameters in Mashup Authoring:
  • Parameter 1: ~transaction = *{transaction_name}%20MS_WERKS- (incorrectly truncated constant)
  • Parameter 2: {transaction_name} = <value> (separated incorrectly)


[Screenshot of Mashup Authoring for Scenario 2 (Wrong Configuration)]

✅ The Correct Configuration (Manual Entry)

To resolve this, do not use the extraction button. Write the complete logical URL in the URL field, and manually define the curly brace parameter in the table below.

  • URL in Mashup Authoring: 
https://example.com/sap/bc/gui/sap/its/webgui?~transaction=*{transaction_name}%20MS_WERKS-LOW=10​
  • Parameters in Mashup Authoring:
  • Parameter 1: {transaction_name} = MM60

 

Scenario 3: Complex Fiori Deep Links (Manual Binding Required)

The most common failure point for the parameter extraction tool involves complex OData or Fiori deep links. These URLs frequently use parentheses to group multiple keys (e.g., Entity(Key1='A',Key2='B')).

The Goal:

We want to deep link into a Payment Advice management app, passing multiple dynamic keys simultaneously.

Expected Mashup URL:

https://example.com/ui?sap-ushell-config=headerless#PaymentAdvice-manage&/C_PaymentAdvice(PaymentAdvice='12',PaymentAdviceAccount='34',CompanyCode='56',PaymentAdviceAccountType='78',DraftUUID=guid'90',IsActiveEntity=true)

❌ The Incorrect Configuration (Using Extract Parameters)

Because there are multiple = signs inside the parentheses, the extraction tool treats them as standard query parameters. It shreds the URL fragment, extracting the UI configuration and OData paths as if they were parameter keys, which generates an invalid URL.

  • URL in Mashup Authoring: 
https://example.com/ui?sap-ushell-config=headerless#PaymentAdvice-manage&/C_PaymentAdvice(PaymentAdvice='{PA}',PaymentAdviceAccount='{PAA}',CompanyCode='{CC}',PaymentAdviceAccountType='{PAAT}',DraftUUID=guid'{uuid}',IsActiveEntity={active})​
  • Parameters in Mashup Authoring (Corrupted):
  • Parameter 1: sap-ushell-config = headerless#PaymentAdvice-manage
  • Parameter 2: /C_PaymentAdvice(PaymentAdvice = '{PA}',PaymentAdviceAccount
  • Parameter 3: {PA} = 12
  • Parameter 4: {PAA} = 34
  • Parameter 5: {CC} = 56
  • Parameter 6: {PAAT} = 78
  • Parameter 7: {uuid} = 90
  • Parameter 8: {active} = true

[Screenshot of Mashup Authoring for Scenario 3 (Wrong Configuration)]

✅ The Correct Configuration (Manual Entry)

Keep the complete, formatted URL intact within the URL definition field, and manually add only the curly brace parameters into the binding table.

  • URL in Mashup Authoring: 
https://example.com/ui?sap-ushell-config=headerless#PaymentAdvice-manage&/C_PaymentAdvice(PaymentAdvice='{PA}',PaymentAdviceAccount='{PAA}',CompanyCode='{CC}',PaymentAdviceAccountType='{PAAT}',DraftUUID=guid'{uuid}',IsActiveEntity={active})​
  • Parameters in Mashup Authoring:
  • Param1: {PA} = 12
  • Param2: {PAA} = 34
  • Param3: {CC} = 56
  • Param4: {PAAT} = 78
  • Param5: {uuid} = 90
  • Param6: {active} = true

 

Summary

The Extract Parameters button is a great time-saver for simple web URLs with standard ?key=value structures. However, if your URL contains:

  1. Special transaction formatting (~transaction=*...)

  2. Complex Fiori/OData paths with parentheses Entity(Key='Value')

  3. URL fragments (#) that are integral to the routing rather than a simple parameter

...you should bypass the extraction tool. Place your complete URL with {} placeholders in the URL field, and manually add your parameters in the Value Binding table to ensure a clean, working integration.

 

SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 
SAP Service Cloud Version 2

entry.title: Mastering SAPUI5 custom Filter Variants Without a Backend Table
entry.content:
Mastering SAPUI5 Filter Variants Without a Backend Table

Many SAPUI5 developers assume that filter variants require a backend table. You might have even thought: “How can I save user filter preferences without touching a database?”

Here’s the good news: you absolutely can, and it’s cleaner, faster, and fully Fiori-compliant. Using SmartVariantManagement (SVM) and a FilterBar, you can build full variant management entirely client-side, while supporting complex controls like MultiInput, MultiComboBox, and DateRangeSelection.

In this blog, we’ll walk through a real-world implementation and explain why a backend table is optional.

1️⃣ Why Backend Tables Are Not Always Needed

Most SAP examples integrate variants with OData services or SmartTables, which gives the impression that server-side storage is required.

But here’s the truth:

  • SmartVariantManagement can store variants in local browser storage for standalone apps.
  • If your app runs in Fiori Launchpad (FLP), variants are stored in the FLP personalization service.
  • The only requirement is to connect your FilterBar to SVM programmatically.

This means backend persistence is optional, not mandatory.

2️⃣ The View Setup

Here’s a clean XML setup with SVM and a FilterBar:

<smartvariants:SmartVariantManagement
    id="svm"
    showExecuteOnSelection="true"
/>

<fb:FilterBar
    id="filterbarList"
    useToolbar="false"
    search=".onSearch"
    persistencyKey="BidEvaluationFilterBar"
    showFilterConfiguration="true"
    afterVariantLoad=".onAfterVariantLoad"
/>

Key Points:

  • showExecuteOnSelection="true" ensures users can auto-execute searches when a variant is selected.
  • persistencyKey allows local persistence even without backend tables.
  • The FilterBar does not automatically connect to SVM; we handle that in the controller.

3️⃣ Wiring FilterBar to SVM in the Controller

3.1 Register Fetch and Apply Functions

this._oFilterBar.registerFetchData(this._fetchVariantData.bind(this));
this._oFilterBar.registerApplyData(this._applyVariantData.bind(this));
this._oFilterBar.attachAfterVariantLoad(this._onAfterVariantLoad.bind(this));

  • _fetchVariantData() extracts all filter values into JSON.
  • _applyVariantData() restores values when a variant is selected.
  • afterVariantLoad ensures auto-search or other post-load logic runs.

3.2 Add FilterBar as Personalizable Control

const oPersInfo = new PersonalizableInfo({
    type: "filterBar",
    keyName: "persistencyKey",
    dataSource: "",
    control: this._oFilterBar
});
this._oSmartVariantManagement.addPersonalizableControl(oPersInfo);

  • PersonalizableInfo registers the FilterBar with SVM, enabling full variant management, including Execute on Selection.

3.3 Initialize SmartVariantManagement

this._oSmartVariantManagement.initialise(function () {}, this._oFilterBar);

  • Establishes the connection between SVM and FilterBar.
  • Variants are now fully functional, even without any backend storage.

4️⃣ Handling Complex Filter Controls

Fetch Variant Data : Explicitly fetches all tokens from MultiInput controls so multiple values are captured per filter.

_fetchVariantData() {
    const oData = {};
    this._oFilterBar.getAllFilterItems().forEach(item => {
        const oControl = item.getControl();
        if (!oControl) return;

        if (oControl.isA("sap.m.MultiInput")) {
            oData[item.getName()] = oControl.getTokens().map(t => t.getKey() || t.getText());
        }

        if (oControl.isA("sap.m.DateRangeSelection")) {
            oData[item.getName()] = {
                from: oControl.getDateValue(),
                to: oControl.getSecondDateValue()
            };
        }

        if (oControl.isA("sap.m.MultiComboBox")) {
            oData[item.getName()] = oControl.getSelectedKeys();
        }
    });
    return oData;
}

Apply Variant Data : Ensures all stored tokens are re-applied, supporting multi-value filter selection.

_applyVariantData(oData) {
    if (!oData) return;

    this._oFilterBar.getAllFilterItems().forEach(item => {
        const oControl = item.getControl();
        const vValue = oData[item.getName()];
        if (!oControl) return;

        if (oControl.isA("sap.m.MultiInput")) {
            oControl.removeAllTokens();
            if (Array.isArray(vValue)) {
                vValue.forEach(val => oControl.addToken(new sap.m.Token({ key: val, text: val })));
            }
        }

        if (oControl.isA("sap.m.DateRangeSelection")) {
            oControl.setDateValue(vValue?.from || null);
            oControl.setSecondDateValue(vValue?.to || null);
        }

        if (oControl.isA("sap.m.MultiComboBox")) {
            oControl.setSelectedKeys(vValue || []);
        }
    });

    // Trigger search automatically
    this.onSearch();
}

  • These functions serialize and restore all filter control values, making the variants robust and accurate.

5️⃣ How It Works (Visual Diagram)

 ┌─────────────────────────────┐
   SmartVariantManagement (SVM)   
   - Variant dialog                                        
   - Execute on Selection                        
   - Stores JSON variants                        
 └─────────────┬───────────────┘
               │
              ▼
 ┌─────────────────────────────┐
   FilterBar                  
   - Displays filter controls

   - Handles token inputs 
   - Registers fetch/apply    
   - Triggers search          
 └─────────────┬───────────────┘
               │
              ▼
 ┌─────────────────────────────┐
   Filter Controls            
    - MultiInput (tokenized)               
   - MultiComboBox            
   - DateRangeSelection       
 └─────────────────────────────┘

Flow Explanation:

  1. FilterBar registers fetch/apply logic for all controls.
  2. PersonalizableInfo connects FilterBar to SVM.
  3. Variants are stored in browser storage (or FLP personalization).
  4. Users can save, load, and auto-execute variants seamlessly.

6️⃣ Key Benefits

  •  Fully functional variant management without a backend table
  • Supports complex controls: MultiInput, MultiComboBox, DateRangeSelection
  • upports complex controls and multi-value filters via tokens
  • Enables Execute on Selection
  • Works in standalone apps or Fiori Launchpad
  • Easy to extend for additional custom controls

7️⃣ Key Takeaways

  1. FilterBar alone is not enough; manual SVM wiring is required.
  2. PersonalizableInfo + addPersonalizableControl() + initialise() connects everything.
  3. Added: Custom fetch/apply functions handle complex controls and **tokenized multi-value filters**.
  4. Custom fetch/apply functions handle complex controls.
  5. Variants can be fully stored client-side, making backend persistence optional.

With this approach, you give users a robust, professional, Fiori-compliant variant experience—without the overhead of backend tables.

entry.title: Re: SAP GUI latest patch
entry.content: Thank you for 600 000 views!
